I see this almost all the time across a variety of different offers and niches, but fitness especially, you guys when you pitch, you do an anvil drop, meaning like you start your pitch and then you talk really fast and really long through all the different elements of your pitch. Then at the very end you're like, okay, well, did that make sense? What questions do you have? They're like totally overwhelmed. They didn't even know what you said. It was hard for them to take it in. They started writing it down, but they quit because you were talking too fast. Your pitch needs to be slow, methodical. There needs to be a lot of tie downs, a lot of opportunities to create dialogue. Your pitch is more of a dialogue than it is an anvil drop. If you take away those two things, guys, I mean that'd be like nobody's really doing this at all and you guys would just dominate. It would help you guys so much. The two key takeaways is for your pitch and fitness. This goes for everybody. Make your pitch the simultaneous explanation of why everything they've tried in the past has failed and why this is going to be different. ## [09:09 - 09:45]

When you have that, it's creating a feedback loop that's allowing you to get a temperature check on where the prospect is so that you're able to create buy-in on the pitch before you transition into the price. Here's the thing with fitness. You need to get them certain that this is the right thing, now is the right time. They're certain that they can see how this is going to be different from everything they tried in the past before you drop price because if you're doing high ticket, especially if you're doing like 5K, when you drop price, totally different ball game on the table. We need those buy-ins up into that point because when we drop price, we're going to lose that. That's all I've got for you guys. Hopefully you enjoy it.
